title: Versatile probe state preparation via generalized measurements for quantum sensing and thermometry
authors: Jonas F. G. Santos, Shanhe Su, Moises Rojas
abstract: We investigate a probe state preparation protocol based on two non-selective generalized quantum measurements to enhance parameter estimation in single-qubit systems. By fine-tuning the measurement strengths, we demonstrate the ability to design a broad class of probe states, initially prepared in a thermal state, which can be optimized for specific estimation tasks. We apply this framework to characterize the decay rate and the temperature of a generalized amplitude damping channel. Our results show that the preparation protocol significantly modulates the quantum Fisher information for both parameters. Furthermore, we derive a general analytical relationship between the quantum Fisher information, thermodynamic susceptibilities, and Hamiltonian variance, valid even in the transient regime. This connection highlights the role of energy fluctuations and kinetic response in determining metrological precision. Finally, we briefly discuss a quantum circuit for experimental implementation using nuclear magnetic resonance techniques.
summary: The paper presents a method to prepare optimized quantum probe states by tuning the strength of generalized measurements. This approach significantly improves the precision of estimating parameters like decay rate and temperature in noisy environments. It also establishes a new connection between quantum metrology and thermodynamic fluctuations in the transient regime.
main_problem: Developing a versatile protocol for preparing optimal probe states via tunable generalized measurements to enhance parameter estimation precision in quantum sensing and thermometry.
main_result: The authors demonstrate that tuning measurement strengths allows for the design of probe states optimized for specific tasks, such as estimating decay rate or temperature, and derive a general analytical relation linking Quantum Fisher Information to thermodynamic susceptibilities and energy variance.
method: The protocol uses a sequence of two non-selective, non-commutative generalized quantum measurements (POVMs) to transform an initial thermal state into a customized probe state.
model_or_system: A single-qubit system (quantum probe) initially in a thermal state, evolving under a Generalized Amplitude Damping (GAD) channel, with a proposed implementation via a four-qubit NMR quantum circuit.
key_observables: Quantum Fisher Information (QFI), thermodynamic susceptibilities (kinetic and thermodynamic), and Hamiltonian variance.
important_parameters: Measurement strengths (p and q), decay rate (Gamma), temperature (T), and interrogation time (t).
main_assumptions: The dynamics are described using the Lindblad master equation under the Born-Markov approximation.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the mapping of measurement strengths to probe states; Fig 2 displays QFI for decay rate and temperature as functions of time and preparation; Fig 3 compares susceptibilities and energy variance; Fig 4 details the proposed NMR pulse sequence.
paper_structure: The paper introduces a probe preparation protocol, applies it to the GAD channel to optimize sensitivity for decay and temperature estimation, derives a fundamental link between metrological precision and thermodynamic fluctuations, and concludes with an experimental implementation scheme.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for open quantum systems and quantum sensing as it provides a practical framework for engineering states to maximize sensitivity to environmental noise and temperature using controllable measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1. The contour plot of Rz (p, q) as functions of p and q, corresponding to different probe state preparation. We have set y0 = 1. While (0, 0) sets the initial thermal state, (1, 0) and (0, 1) define the probe state to be ground and excited state, respectively.

Fig. 2. Quantum Fisher information. a) QFI for the estima- tion of Γ. Here, we assumed yeq = 1 in order to see explicitly the effect of the probe state preparation. b) QFI for the es- timation of T. Here, we defined yeq = 0.5. Both QFIs are function of the interrogation time t and for different probe state preparations defined by R. We have set ℏω = 1.0 and Γ = 0.05.

Fig. 3. a) Kinetic and thermodynamic susceptibility as func- tion of the interrogation time for different probe state prepa- rations. b) Standard deviation as function of the interroga- tion time for different probe state preparations. We have set ℏω = 1.0, T = 1 (in units of ℏω/kB), Γ = 0.05 and y0 = 1.

Fig. 4. Propose of pulses sequences to implement probe state preparation protocol and the AD or GAD channels. For the probe state preparation, we have two nonselective generalized measurements channels, M and N. For M and N, the mea- surement strength parameters p and q are tuned by the angles θ1 and θ2, respectively, with both angles ranging in [0, π/2), according to θ1 = arccos (1 −2p) and θ2 = arccos (1 −2q). The qubits Q2, Q3, and Q4 are not observed, resulting in the desired nonselective operations channels (CPTP maps) acting on the Q1 (probe system). The parameter s is related to the interrogation time t by cos (s) = exp [−Γt/2]. This setup for the quantum circuit is based on Ref....
